titleStmt.title: Integrating Structural and Semantic Signals in Text-Attributed Graphs with BiGTex

affiliation.orgName: Faculty of Electrical and Computer Engineering
Faculty of Electrical and Computer Engineering

analytic.title: Integrating Structural and Semantic Signals in Text-Attributed Graphs with BiGTex

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Text-attributed graphs (TAGs) present unique challenges in representation learning by requiring models to capture both the semantic richness of node-associated texts and the structural dependencies of the graph. While graph neural networks (GNNs) excel at modeling topological information, they lack the capacity to process unstructured text. Conversely, large language models (LLMs) are proficient in text understanding but are typically unaware of graph structure. In this work, we propose BiG-Tex (Bidirectional Graph Text), a novel architecture that tightly integrates GNNs and LLMs through stacked Graph-Text Fusion Units. Each unit allows for mutual attention between textual and structural representations, enabling information to flow in both directions, text influencing structure and structure guiding textual interpretation. The proposed architecture is trained using parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA), keeping the LLM frozen while adapting to task-specific signals. Extensive experiments on five benchmark datasets demonstrate that BiGTex achieves state-of-the-art performance in node classification and generalizes effectively to link prediction. An ablation study further highlights the importance of soft prompting and bi-directional attention in the model's success.

div.head: INTRODUCTION
div.p: Many real-world systems can be effectively modelled as graphs, where nodes represent entities and edges capture the relationships between them. In certain types of graphs, nodes are associated with textual information, such as descriptions, labels, or documents. These structures are commonly referred to as Text-Attributed Graphs (TAGs) [1]. For instance in Figure 1, in citation networks [2], each node represents a scientific paper, which may be accompanied by its title or abstract as textual attributes. Similarly, in social networks [3], nodes typically represent users, each of whom may have a profile containing text-based information like personal traits, preferences, or interests.

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) [4,5] have demonstrated strong capabilities in learning highquality representations from graph-structured data, which are effective in various graph-related tasks such as node classification and link prediction. These models primarily operate based on message passing mechanisms over the graph topology, focusing solely on the structural information. As a result, they inherently overlook any textual attributes associated with the nodes.

On the other hand, pre-trained language models (PLMs) [6] excel at understanding and representing the semantics of natural language texts. When node attributes are textual, PLMs can extract meaningful features from them. However, PLMs alone are not designed to capture the underlying structure of graphs. Therefore, integrating PLMs with GNNs presents a promising direction, as it enables models to leverage both textual and structural aspects of graph data.

Several approaches have been proposed in the integration PLMs and GNNs. Some methods, such as TAPE [7] and SimTEG [8], first utilize a language model to encode node texts into dense representations, which are then used as initial node features for a downstream GNN. In these approaches, the PLM and GNN operate independently; the PLM encodes node texts either with or without fine-tuning, and the resulting embeddings are fed into the GNN without considering the graph structure during text encoding.

Other approaches reverse the process of work: a GNN first computes structural embeddings based on graph connectivity, and these are subsequently combined with the original node texts and passed through a language model (e.g., GraphGPT [9], Tea-GLM [10], and GIMLET [11]). While these methods have also shown promising results, the language and graph components still function independently without joint optimization or mutual awareness.

More recent approaches attempt to bridge this gap by encouraging alignment between textual and structural representations, often through contrastive learning techniques. For instance, models like GAugLLM [12] generate alternative graph views by incorporating PLM-derived embeddings and train the GNN using contrastive objectives to align the original and augmented views.

Despite these advancements, many existing methods either ignore the graph structure during text encoding or fail to update the language model in a graph-aware manner [13]. This highlights a need for tighter integration between GNNs and PLMs to enable more effective joint representation learning.

Our proposed architecture leverages the strengths of both language models and graph neural networks by aligning their representations to produce enriched node embeddings for text-attributed graphs (TAGs). The architecture is composed of modular blocks, each integrating a pre-trained language model with a GNN layer in a mutually attentive fashion.

Specifically, the output of the GNN layer is used as an additional input token to the language model, placed alongside the textual content of each node. This design encourages the language model to become aware of the graph structure. Simultaneously, the output of the language model is fused with the GNN representation through a cross-attention mechanism, allowing the GNN to incorporate semantic information from the node texts.

This bidirectional interaction between the two components fosters joint learning of structure-aware and text-sensitive representations. Experimental results show that our model consistently achieves strong performance on node classification tasks, whether the language model is fine-tuned or kept frozen during training. Our main contributions are summarized as follows:

1. We introduce BiGTex, a novel hybrid architecture, that integrates pre-trained language models with graph neural network layers in a bi-directional attention scheme. The proposed model is designed to generate effective representations for text-attributed graphs (TAGs) by simultaneously leveraging the semantic understanding of PLMs and the structural reasoning of GNNs.

2. Unlike previous methods, our architecture implements a distinct form of dual attention within each block. Specifically, the structural output of the GNN is injected into the language model as an additional token, enabling the language model to attend to graph structure. Meanwhile, the output of the language model is refined through a cross-attention layer that operates on the GNN embeddings, allowing the graph encoder to integrate semantic cues from text.

3. We conduct extensive experiments across a diverse set of TAG benchmarks. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, with consistent improvements in performance. Notably, on the Arxiv dataset, our method achieves a significant accuracy gain of 14.2%, highlighting its practical utility in real-world graph-based tasks.

div.head: PRELIMINARIES
div.p: In this section, we provide an overview of the key concepts and background knowledge relevant to our proposed framework. It is begun by defining text-attributed graphs (TAGs) and tasks in such graphs.

Then briefly introducing the foundational principles of graph neural networks (GNNs) and pre-trained language models (PLMs), followed by a discussion on fine-tuning strategies commonly applied to PLMs in downstream tasks.

div.head: Text-Attributed Graph
div.p: A text-attributed graph can be formally represented as G = (V , E, {S n } n∈V , Y ), where V denotes the set of nodes, E ⊆ V × V the set of edges, and {S n } a collection of text sequences associated with the nodes. Each node n ∈ V is coupled not only with structural features (e.g., neighbourhood connectivity or optional node attributes), but also with a textual description S n , which is a variable-length sequence of tokens of length L n . The label space Y contains node-level annotations for a subset of nodes in V , while the remaining nodes are unlabelled [7].

div.head: Task Definitions
div.p: In the context of text-attributed graphs, a variety of learning tasks can be formulated based on different supervision levels and application goals. Below, we outline three fundamental tasks commonly studied in this setting.

Node Classification: The primary task addressed in this work is node classification, where the goal is to learn a predictive function f : V → Y that generalizes from labelled nodes to unlabelled ones, using both the graph structure and the associated textual information. Given a partially labelled graph G, the objective is to infer the correct labels for the unlabelled nodes based on their position in the graph and the content of their associated texts.

Link Prediction: Link prediction focuses on estimating the likelihood of missing or future connections between node pairs in a graph. In a text-attributed setting, this task involves predicting whether an edge should exist between two nodes based on both their structural context (e.g., proximity, common neighbors) and the semantic similarity of their associated text sequences. This task is especially relevant in applications such as recommendation systems, knowledge graph completion, and social network analysis.

Node Clustering: Node clustering is an unsupervised task aimed at grouping nodes into clusters such that nodes within the same group exhibit similar structural roles or semantic properties. In textattributed graphs, this involves jointly leveraging the graph topology and the textual content to identify coherent communities or categories of nodes. Unlike classification, clustering does not rely on labelled data and is often used for exploratory data analysis, visualization, or pre-training of downstream models.

div.head: Graph Neural networks (GNNs)
div.p: Graph Neural Networks [4,5] (GNNs) have emerged as powerful tools for learning expressive representations from graph-structured data. They have proven effective in various tasks such as node classification, link prediction, and graph-level inference. The core mechanism behind most GNNs is based on iterative message passing, where each node updates its representation by aggregating information from its local neighbourhood.

Formally, let N n denote the set of neighbours of node n. At each layer k, the representation h k n of node i is computed by aggregating the representations of its neighbours from the previous layer k -1 and applying an update function:
div.formula: h k n = f k (h k-1 n , AGG h k-1 j : j ∈ N n ) ∈ R d (1)
div.p: Here, AGG represents a permutation-invariant aggregation function, such as mean, sum, or max pooling. The function f can be instantiated using a multilayer perceptron, an attention mechanism, or other differentiable transformations. By stacking multiple GNN layers, nodes are able to incorporate information from multi-hop neighbourhoods, enabling the model to capture richer context within the graph structure.

div.head: Large language Models (LLMs) and Attention Mechanism
div.p: Large Language Models (LLMs) are powerful deep learning architectures developed for large-scale natural language processing tasks such as text generation, summarization, and classification. These models are typically built on transformer architectures, which avoid sequential computation and instead rely on self-attention mechanisms to model long-range dependencies in language efficiently and in parallel.

A central component in transformers is the attention mechanism, which allows the model to dynamically weigh the importance of different input tokens when computing contextualized representations. This is achieved by transforming each token into three distinct vectors: queries (Q), keys (K), and values (V). The attention weights are calculated using a scaled dot-product function, defined as:
div.formula: Attention (Q, K, V ) = sof tmax       QK d k       V (2)
div.p: Here, d k is the dimensionality of the key vectors, used for scaling to stabilize gradients during training. This formulation allows each token to attend selectively to all others in the input sequence based on learned relevance scores [6].

To capture different types of relationships simultaneously, the model uses multi-head attention, where multiple independent attention operations are performed in parallel. The outputs of these "heads" are then concatenated and linearly projected to form the final attention output. This design enriches the model's ability to represent diverse semantic and syntactic patterns within text.

LLMs are typically trained in two phases: a pretraining phase and a fine-tuning phase [14]. During pretraining, the model is exposed to massive corpora using self-supervised objectives such as masked language modelling or causal prediction, allowing it to learn general linguistic patterns without labelled data [15]. In the fine-tuning stage, the pretrained model is adapted to specific tasks using smaller, task-specific datasets. Depending on the architecture, this phase may involve full model tuning or lightweight approaches such as adapter layers [16] or LoRA [17] (Low-Rank Adaptation).

The synergy between attention mechanisms and large-scale training enables LLMs to extract deep semantic representations, making them highly effective for a broad range of downstream applicationsincluding the one explored in this work.

Continuing from the architectural foundations of large language models, it is important to distinguish between two major classes of transformer-based designs: encoder-only and decoder-only models. Each follows a unique training paradigm and serves different purposes in natural language understanding or generation tasks.

Encoder-only models, such as BERT [18], are designed to generate holistic representations of input sequences. These models take an entire sequence as input and process it bidirectionally-each token has access to the full left and right context during training. This characteristic allows the model to deeply capture semantic dependencies across the input.

The standard training objective for such models is Masked Language Modelling (MLM), where a random subset of tokens is replaced with a special [MASK] token. The model is then trained to reconstruct the masked tokens using the surrounding unmasked tokens. This approach encourages the network to develop contextualized embeddings grounded in full-sequence comprehension. The loss function for MLM is typically expressed as:
div.formula: L MLM = - iǫM logP (x i | x \M ; θ) (3)
div.p: where \M denotes the set of masked positions, x \M is the unmasked sequence, and θ represents model parameters.

In contrast, decoder-only models, such as GPT [19], operate unidirectionally. They are trained in an autoregressive fashion, where each token is generated based only on preceding tokens. The model has no access to future inputs during training or inference, enforcing a strict left-to-right dependency. This makes them particularly well-suited for generative tasks such as open-ended text generation or dialogue modelling.

The pretraining objective for decoder-only models is Causal Language Modelling (CLM), where the model learns to predict the next token in a sequence, given all previous ones. The associated loss is defined as:
div.formula: L CLM = - T t=1 logP(x t |x <t ; θ) (4)
div.p: Here, each token x t is conditioned solely on the sequence x <t , making the model capable of generating coherent text step by step.

The distinction between encoder-only and decoder-only models is foundational: the former are optimized for understanding input context and are widely used in classification and embedding tasks, while the latter are tailored for sequential prediction and text generation. Choosing between them depends on the nature of the task at hand-whether the goal is to comprehend a complete input or to generate output in a temporally coherent manner.

Training large language models (LLMs) from scratch is a resource-intensive process, often requiring days of computation across thousands of GPUs and substantial financial investment. As an alternative, fine-tuning pre-trained models has become a widely adopted practice. This approach adapts generalpurpose models to specific downstream tasks by continuing training on task-specific data, offering a practical balance between performance and efficiency.

To further reduce the cost of adaptation, Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) techniques have been proposed. These methods update only a small subset of parameters while keeping the majority of the pre-trained model frozen, significantly cutting down memory and compute requirements. Among the most effective PEFT methods is LoRA [17] (Low-Rank Adaptation), which introduces trainable lowrank matrices into existing weight structures.
Instead of updating the full weight matrix w O ∈ R d×k , LoRA approximates the update as a low-rank product:
div.formula: ∆W = BA B ∈ R d×r , A ∈ R r×k , r ≪ min(d, k)(5)
div.p: This allows the model to adapt to new tasks with minimal parameter updates, while preserving the knowledge encoded in the original model.
In our study, we utilize LoRA as the primary fine-tuning method, enabling efficient adaptation to classification tasks while significantly lowering training costs and resource demands.

div.head: Related Work
div.p: The intersection of natural language processing and graph representation learning has given rise to a variety of hybrid approaches for encoding structured data with textual attributes. This section reviews key developments in this evolving area, focusing on how large language models (LLMs) have been leveraged-either independently or in conjunction with graph neural networks (GNNs)-to produce expressive node representations.

The existing literatures are categorized into three main directions. First, Representation Learning with only Large Language Models, explores methods that solely rely on LLMs for representation learning over text-attributed graphs, emphasizing their ability to extract semantic-rich embeddings from node texts (3.1). Next, Sequential Integration of GNNs and LLMs, that examine sequential architectures where GNNs and LLMs are applied in a pipeline, with one model generating features that are subsequently refined by the other (3.2). Finally, Joint Representation Learning with GNN-LLM Integration, discusses more recent efforts that attempt a deeper integration of both paradigms, enabling joint learning and mutual adaptation between structural and textual representations (3.3).

This categorization provides a comprehensive view of the strengths and limitations of current strategies, and positions our proposed framework in the broader context of representation learning on textattributed graphs.

div.head: Representation Learning with only Large Language Models
div.p: A growing body of research has explored the use of large language models (LLMs) for learning node representations in text-attributed graphs (TAGs) without incorporating any form of graph-specific in-ductive bias. In this line of work, LLMs serve as the sole representation learners, relying purely on textual node attributes while ignoring the underlying graph topology. Some approaches apply LLMs in a zero-shot or frozen setting, using their pretrained capacities to encode node texts without any task-specific fine-tuning [20,21,22]. For instance, NLGraph [20] repurposes LLMs for reasoning over graph-structured data through natural language, addressing tasks such as connectivity checks, cycle detection, and shortest path estimation-without relying on graph neural networks.

Other studies have fine-tuned LLMs for downstream graph-related tasks to enhance their taskawareness and representation quality [23,24,25]. For example, GraphWiz [24] first generates taskspecific datasets using LLMs by prompting them to create synthetic examples, and then fine-tunes the model using optimization methods such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), resulting in better alignment with the target objectives.

Although these methods have shown impressive results in language-centric tasks, they do not incorporate graph structure directly. Some variants-like MuseGraph [26]-attempt to inject structural context by linearizing graph neighbourhoods into token sequences. However, due to the token length limitations inherent to transformer-based LLMs, fully encoding large or complex graphs remains.

div.head: Sequential Integration of GNNs and LLMs
div.p: A number of studies have explored sequential strategies for combining Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and Large Language Models (LLMs), where the two components are applied in a fixed pipeline rather than in a jointly optimized manner. These methods aim to utilize both the semantic richness of LLMs and the structural awareness of GNNs, but often fall short in fully capturing the synergy between them.

One class of approaches first leverages an LLM to encode the textual attributes of each node into dense embeddings. These embeddings are then used as initial node features for a GNN, which incorporates structural information to produce final representations. Examples of this architecture include TAPE [7] and SimTeG [8], which use transformer-based language models to extract context-aware embeddings from node texts before feeding them into a GNN for downstream tasks.

In contrast, other works reverse the sequence: a GNN is first employed to encode graph structure into node-level representations, which are then passed into an LLM-either concatenated with node texts or used as prompts. For instance, GraphGPT [9], Tea-GLM [10], and GIMLET [11] follow this structure, where GNN-derived embeddings enrich the language model input, enabling the model to generate more informed outputs.

Although these serial architectures incorporate both text and topology, they often process them in isolation. Because the two components are not trained in a unified or interactive manner, such models may struggle to fully exploit the interdependencies between textual and structural modalitiespotentially limiting performance on graph-centric tasks like node classification or link prediction.

div.head: Joint Representation Learning with GNN-LLM Integration
div.p: Integrating structural and textual signals in text-attributed graphs (TAGs) remains a critical challenge in representation learning. Unlike sequential methods that process language and structure in isolation, recent approaches aim to establish tighter interactions between graph neural networks (GNNs) and large language models (LLMs), allowing both components to influence each other during training.

GLEM [27] proposes an Expectation-Maximization (EM)-based framework in which the GNN and LLM are trained in alternating steps. This design enables both models to iteratively refine each other's outputs without incurring the full cost of co-training, leading to better utilization of both structural and semantic information.

In a different direction, GraphAdapter [28] introduces a lightweight GNN adapter that operates on top of a frozen LLM. By injecting graph-aware representations with minimal overhead, the model enhances the LLM's ability to handle graph-structured inputs, while maintaining compatibility with zero-shot scenarios.

Another recent work, ENGINE [29], presents an efficient training scheme for TAGs through a parallel structure called G-Ladder, which augments the LLM with a separate graph message-passing branch. While the language model remains fixed, the auxiliary GNN pathway learns structural representations that can be cached and reused. Additionally, the method incorporates dynamic early exiting to reduce inference latency. These approaches reflect a growing trend toward synergistic LM-GNN frameworks that emphasize scalability, efficiency, and deeper modality fusion. They pave the way for more integrated architectures capable of jointly capturing textual meaning and graph topology in a unified representation space.

div.head: Remarks
div.p: Despite the progress achieved by recent approaches in leveraging language models and graph neural networks for TAG representation learning, several limitations remain. Methods that treat the two modalities in isolation-either through independent processing or fixed pipelines-often fail to fully exploit their complementary strengths. Even in joint models, challenges such as limited cross-modal interaction, scalability constraints, or reliance on frozen components can restrict the expressive power of the learned embeddings. Motivated by these gaps, we introduce a novel hybrid framework designed to tightly integrate LLMs and GNNs through mutual attention, enabling more effective and context-aware representations for text-attributed graphs.

div.head: Proposed framework: BiGTex
div.p: In this section, we present our proposed framework, which integrates Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate rich and context-aware node representations for textattributed graphs (TAGs). BiGTex is designed to facilitate deep interaction between textual and structural modalities through a tightly coupled architecture that enables mutual information exchange.

The overall structure of the framework is first outlined, followed by a detailed explanation of its Graph-Text Fusion units (4.1) and training strategy (4.2). A schematic overview of the proposed architecture is illustrated in Figure 2, where the key modules and the flow of information between the LLM and GNN components are highlighted.

div.head: Graph-Text Fusion Architecture
div.p: Our proposed architecture is composed of a stack of sequential blocks, each referred to as a Graph-Text Fusion Unit, where both a Graph Neural Network (GNN) component and a Pretrained Language Model (PLM) module are jointly employed. As illustrated in Figure 2, each unit receives three inputs:
1) The adjacency matrix of the graph (or the edge list, A in Figure 2), 2) The textual descriptions associated with the graph's nodes (text in Figure 2), and

3) The output representations generated by the preceding Fusion Unit. For the first unit in the stack, the initial node features (typically derived from shallow methods such as Skip-gram, Bag-of-Words, or TF-IDF) are used in place of the previous unit's output (x in Figure 2).

Within each Graph-Text Fusion Unit, the output vector from the preceding unit is treated as a soft structural token and prepended to the sequence of input tokens corresponding to the node's textual content. A special [SEP] token is inserted between the structural vector and the original text tokens to clearly separate the modalities. This modified input sequence is then passed into the PLM, which computes contextualized token embeddings via its attention mechanism.

Importantly, by inserting the structural token at the beginning of the sequence, we allow the PLM to attend to graph-based structural information during self-attention. In this way, the model incorporates graph awareness through a soft-prompting strategy, without modifying the internal parameters of the PLM itself.

Let o l-1 ∈ R d PLM represent the output vector of node n from the l -1-th Fusion Unit. This vector is prepended to the tokenized textual sequence of the node, forming an enriched input to the language model for the next layer. This approach facilitates progressive refinement of node embeddings through layered interaction between textual semantics and structural cues.
div.formula: Prompt PLM n = [o l-1 || [sep] || emb({S n })](6)
div.p: Let emb( {S n } ) denote the embedding output of the token sequence corresponding to the textual content of node n, and let || represent the concatenation operation. As described earlier, the structural token o l-1 , obtained from the previous Graph-Text Fusion Unit, is concatenated with the textual embeddings to form the input prompt to the language model. This input enables the PLM to attend not only to the semantic content of the node's text but also to the graph-aware token. Through self-attention, the PLM captures how the structural prompt influences the interpretation of textual tokens. The result of this process (typically extracted as the output of the special [CLS] token in encoder-based PLMs) serves as a text-conditioned representation enriched with structural context. In the subsequent step, we reverse the flow of interaction: the structural encoder (GNN) now attends to the text-informed representations. To enable this, we introduce a cross-attention layer, where the query matrix is derived from the output of the previous GNN layer, while the key and value matrices originate from the PLM's output:
div.formula: [Cross -Attention (Q, K, V ) = sof tmax       QK d k       V (7)
div.p: where
div.formula: K, V = PLM(Prompt PLM n ) and Q = o l-1
div.p: This mechanism enables the graph representation to be refined using the semantic context extracted by the language model. The resulting vector encodes both neighbourhood structure and text-based meaning in a coherent, node-specific embedding.

Finally, this cross-attended feature vector is passed-along with the graph's adjacency matrix-into the GNN module within the current Graph-Text Fusion Unit. The GNN then updates the node's representation by aggregating information from its neighbours, now enriched by both structural proximity and contextual textual signals. The output of this GNN layer becomes the final output of the current Fusion Unit and is passed to the next unit in the stack (if any).

div.head: Training Strategy and Fine-Tuning
div.p: The PLM module in each Graph-Text Fusion Unit can be instantiated with any pretrained language model. If the model is encoder-only-such as BERT-we use the embedding corresponding to the [CLS] token as the output. For decoder-only models like GPT, we instead use the average of the hidden states across all tokens as the representation.

Given that our target task is node classification, we append a lightweight classification headimplemented as a simple multi-layer perceptron (MLP)-to the output of the final Fusion Unit. This head maps the learned node embeddings to class logits.

During training, the GNN layers are fully trainable. For the PLM component, we adopt a parameterefficient fine-tuning [30] (PEFT) approach using LoRA [17] (Low-Rank Adaptation). In this setup, the pretrained weights of the language model remain untouched, while small, trainable low-rank matrices are introduced to enable adaptation to the task. This avoids catastrophic forgetting and significantly reduces the computational cost, allowing the PLM to specialize for the classification task without requiring full fine-tuning.

In the first Fusion Unit, since the initial node features x may not be dimensionally aligned with the PLM's hidden space d PLM, we apply a linear projection to map the raw input into the appropriate embedding space. This projection ensures compatibility between the initial input and the PLM-GNN fusion process.
The overall training objective is formulated as a standard cross-entropy loss [31] applied to the classifier's output over labelled nodes:
div.formula: Loss cls = L θ (head o K , Y )(8)
div.p: where o K is the output representation from the final Fusion Unit, Y denotes the ground truth labels, and θ includes all trainable parameters: the GNN weights, the LoRA adapters injected into the PLM, cross-attention layer and the parameters of the classification head.

div.head: Experiments and Results
div.p: This section presents the experimental setup and evaluates the effectiveness of the proposed model across a range of graph-based tasks. Four experiments are designed to systematically explore the following research questions:
1. Does the proposed architecture lead to measurable improvements in node-level performance compared to standard baselines?
To what extent does incorporating a language model alongside a GNN enhance the overall representational power of the model?
3. How does fine-tuning the language model, even under parameter-efficient settings, influence the quality of the learned embeddings?
4. Are the embeddings generated by the proposed method also effective in other tasks, such as link prediction or clustering?
Through these questions, we aim to gain a deeper understanding of the model's behavior under different configurations and its potential advantages over existing approaches.

div.head: Experimental Setup
div.p: All experiments were conducted under a unified training configuration to ensure fairness and comparability across models. The learning rate was fixed at 0.0001, with a weight decay of 1e-5 applied during optimization. A mini-batch size of 6 was used throughout, and batches were constructed using the NeighborLoader2 module for efficient subgraph sampling. Throughout all experiments, we employ BERT-Base [18] as the default pre-trained encoder unless otherwise specified. Language model parameters were frozen during training, and fine-tuning was performed using the LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) method with rank=8, allowing for lightweight and parameter-efficient updates. All experiments were executed using PyG 2.5, and computations were performed on a GeForce RTX 4090 GPU. This configuration was consistently applied across all datasets and models to maintain a controlled evaluation environment. For full implementation details, hyperparameter settings, and code, please refer to our public project page3 .

div.head: Datasets
div.p: Given that our primary focus is on the node classification task, we evaluate BiGTex on four benchmark datasets widely used in graph learning research: ogbn-Arxiv [33], ogbn-Products (subset) [33], PubMed [34], and Arxiv2023 [7]. These datasets offer diverse characteristics in terms of graph size, domain, and textual richness, making them suitable for assessing the generalizability of our approach across different settings. For these datasets, we used raw text data collected in [7]. For ogbn-Arxiv and ogbn-Products, we follow the official data splits provided by the Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) [33]. For PubMed and Arxiv2023, we adopt a standard 60/20/20 split for training, validation, and testing respectively. A summary of their key statistics is provided in Table 1. There are some descriptions of dataset in Appendix A.

div.head: Baselines
div.p: To evaluate the effectiveness of our proposed model, we compare it against nine baseline methods, grouped into two main categories:

1. GNN-based baselines (MLP&GNNs): This category includes classical graph neural network architectures such as GCN [4], GAT [35], and GraphSAGE [5]. These models operate solely on structural and non-textual node features and serve as a reference for evaluating the added benefit of incorporating textual information. Their performance is based on the original node features provided with each dataset (as described in Table 1).

div.head: LLM-enhanced graph models (GNN&LLM):
div.p: The second group consists of recent state-of-the-art methods that combine large language models with GNNs, either in a sequential or integrated manner. This category includes GAINT [36], TAPE [7], SimTeG [8], GLEM [37] and ENGINE [29].
All of which have demonstrated strong performance on various TAG benchmarks by leveraging both textual and structural signals.
These baselines allow us to assess not only how our method performs relative to classical GNNs but also how it stands against modern hybrid architectures that utilize pretrained language models.

div.head: Main results (Node classification task)
div.p: The core experimental results are summarized in Table 2, where we report the test set accuracies for all models across the selected datasets. All reported values are the mean and standard deviation over two independent runs, each trained for 10 epochs using two stacked Graph-Text Fusion Units in our architecture.

As highlighted in the Table 2, our proposed model consistently outperforms the baselines on most datasets. It demonstrates a substantial accuracy gain over traditional GNN-based methods, clearly indicating the benefit of combining graph structure with language-based semantic reasoning. Moreover, when compared to the LLM-enhanced baselines, our model also shows notable improvements. Specifically, we observe an increase of 14.2% in accuracy on the ogbn-Arxiv dataset, a 1.89% gain on OGBN-Products and 2.91% On the PubMed dataset, relative to state-of-the-art methods such as TAPE and SimTeG. These findings strongly support the effectiveness of our bi-directional interaction strategy, in which both text influence's structure and structure influences text. This dual-attention mechanism enables more context-aware and semantically enriched representations, leading to improved performance in node classification tasks on TAGs. X-orig X-BiGTex X-TAPE X-SimTeG

div.head: Ablation Study
div.p: To investigate the individual impact of key components in our architecture, we conduct an ablation study by selectively removing each element and evaluating the resulting model performance. The results are summarized in Table 4. Our full model, referred to as BiGTex, achieves the highest accuracy across all five datasets, confirming the effectiveness of the complete architecture. When LoRA-based fine-tuning is disabled (w/o LoRA), we observe a moderate drop in performance, especially on more complex datasets like OGBN-Arxiv (from 88.51% to 79.61%) and Products (from 89.01% to 81.98%). This shows that LoRA plays a crucial role in adapting the language model to graph-based tasks with minimal overhead. Removing the soft prompt mechanism (w/o soft prompt) leads to a significant performance decline, highlighting the importance of injecting structural information directly into the language model's input space. Notably, when both LoRA and soft prompting are omitted (w/o soft prompt & LoRA), the performance degrades drastically across all datasets-dropping as low as 44.58% on Products. This confirms that the bidirectional interaction between text and structure-enabled by both components-is vital for effective representation learning. Overall, the ablation results underscore the complementary nature of LoRA and soft prompting, and demonstrate that their combination is key to the model's success.

div.head: Leaderboard for ogbn-Arxiv
div.p: To benchmark the performance of our model against established state-of-the-art approaches, we compare BiGTex with top-ranking methods listed on the Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) leaderboard for the OGBN-Arxiv dataset 4 . This comparison includes metrics on both the validation and test sets, as well as the number of trainable parameters and GPU hardware used in each method. As shown in Table 5, our model achieves the highest test accuracy among all reported methods, outperforming models such as GLEM, SimTeG, and TAPE. Notably, BiGTex reaches a test accuracy of 88.51%, surpassing the previous top result with a significant margin. Additionally, our model maintains a relatively low number of trainable parameters compared to several heavyweight architectures and was trained efficiently on a single GeForce RTX 4090 GPU. These results demonstrate the strong scalability, effectiveness, and practical applicability of our model. The ability to outperform leaderboard baselines using modest computational resources highlights the value of our bi-directional fusion design and parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategy.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: In this paper, we introduced BiGTex, a hybrid architecture that combines the strengths of graph neural networks and large language models through deep, bidirectional interaction. By integrating a soft structural prompt into the PLM input and using cross-attention to propagate textual cues back into the GNN, our model enables rich and task-relevant node representations to emerge. Experimental results across a variety of TAG datasets demonstrate that BiGTex not only surpasses both traditional GNNs and existing LLM-enhanced baselines but also produces embeddings that transfer well to other tasks such as link prediction. Our ablation analysis confirms the complementary roles of structural prompting and parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Future work may extend this framework to dynamic or heterogeneous graphs, or explore integration with instruction-tuned language models for improved generalization.

div.head: Limitations and Future Work
div.p: While our proposed model achieves strong results across multiple benchmarks, it is not without limitations. First, although the use of soft prompts and LoRA significantly reduces training costs, the overall computational requirements remain relatively high-especially when stacking multiple Graph-Text Fusion Units or processing large-scale graphs with long textual content. This may limit the practicality of the model in resource-constrained environments.

Second, our approach assumes that node-level text is available and semantically rich, which may not hold in all real-world graph scenarios. The performance of the model may degrade when textual features are sparse, noisy, or uninformative. Furthermore, our current design is tailored for static, homogeneous graphs. Adapting the architecture to dynamic, heterogeneous, or multi-relational graphs remains an open challenge, and doing so may require more advanced fusion and attention mechanisms.

For future research, we aim to (1) explore scalable variants of our model that can better handle long documents and extremely large graphs, (2) incorporate instruction-tuned or domain-adapted language models to improve performance in specialized applications, and (3) investigate semi-supervised or fewshot variants to better support low-label regimes. though the model was not explicitly trained for clustering, these results highlight the strong unsupervised discriminative capacity of the embeddings generated by BiGTex. Table 6: Clustering performance (ARI and NMI) of node embeddings from different models on the OGBN-Arxiv and Arxiv2023. BiGTex consistently yields the most class-consistent cluster structures, outperforming TAPE and original features.

figure.head: Figure 2 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 2: Overview of the proposed architecture (BiGTex). The left side illustrates the overall model structure, where multiple Graph-Text Fusion Units (blocks) are stacked to process input node features and generate final representations. The right side provides a detailed view of a single Fusion Unit, showcasing the internal interactions between the GNN and LLM components. This unit employs mutual attention mechanisms to enable bidirectional information exchange between structural and textual modalities, facilitating joint representation learning.

figure.head: Figure 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 3: t-SNE visualization of node representations for ogbn-Arxiv and Arxiv2023 datasets. Each plot shows a 2D projection of 100 randomly sampled nodes per class. From left to right: original input features (X-orig), embeddings generated by SimTeG [8] (X-SimTeG), embeddings generated by TAPE [7] (X-TAPE) and embeddings generated by our proposed model (X-BiGTex). Compared to the baselines, our method produces more distinct and well-separated clusters, indicating stronger classlevel discrimination in the learned representations.

figure.head: Table 2 :
figure.figDesc: Test accuracy of all models across five text-attributed graph datasets. Each result is reported as the mean ± standard deviation over two runs, using two Graph-Text Fusion Units and 10 training epochs. The best result for each dataset is bolded, while the second-best is underlined.

figure.head: Table 4 :
figure.figDesc: Ablation study results showing the test accuracy (%) of the full model (BiGTex) and its reduced variants across five datasets. Removing either LoRA or soft prompt leads to noticeable performance drops, while omitting both causes severe degradation.

figure.head: Table 5 :
figure.figDesc: Comparison of BiGTex with top-performing models on the OGBN-Arxiv leaderboard. Metrics include validation/test accuracy, number of trainable parameters, and GPU hardware used. BiGTex achieves the top test accuracy with fewer resources.

figure.head: Table 6 :
figure.figDesc: Clustering performance (ARI and NMI) of node embeddings from different models on the OGBN-Arxiv and Arxiv2023. BiGTex consistently yields the most class-consistent cluster structures, outperforming TAPE and original features.

note.p: * Corresponding author. Email: vahidipour@kashanu.ac.ir. Email(s): a.Beiranvand@grad.kashanu.ac.ir (Azadeh Beiranvand), vahidipour@kashanu.ac.ir (Seyed Mehdi Vahidipour)
N eighborLoader is a minibatch sampling utility provided in the PyT orch Geometric library. See : https : //pytorchgeometric.readthedocs.io/en/2.6.1/ modules/torch geometric/loader/neighbor loader.
https : //ogb.stanf ord.edu/docs/leader nodeprop/#ogbnarxiv, accessed on April 16, 2025, 10 : 30 AM.

div.head: Visualizing Embedding Quality with t-SNE
div.p: To qualitatively evaluate the discriminative power of the learned embeddings, we use t-SNE [38] to project the high-dimensional node representations into a 2D space. For each dataset, we randomly sample 100 nodes per class and visualize them as individual points, color-coded by class. As illustrated in Figure 3, we compare three types of representations:

1. X-orig: the original input features provided by the dataset, 2. X-SimTeG: node embeddings obtained from the SimTeG [8] The t-SNE plots are shown for two datasets: ogbn-Arxiv and Arxiv2023. In both cases, embeddings produced by our model exhibit clearer class separation compared to those of the others. This observation suggests that our model effectively integrates both textual and structural information, resulting in more informative and separable representations in the latent space.

div.head: Link Prediction Task
div.p: To assess the versatility of the learned representations, we further evaluate our model on a link prediction task. Specifically, we reuse the node embeddings generated by our model (originally trained for node classification) and apply them to predict the existence of edges between node pairs. The performance is measured using the Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) metric. As shown in Table 3, we compare the results of our method with those of SimTeG and TAPE, strong baselines that also integrate language models with GNNs. Despite the fact that our embeddings were obtained through supervision on a classification task, they exhibit competitive AUC scores on the link prediction task. These results highlight the generalizability of our model: the dual-modality representations it produces are not only effective for node classification, but also transferable to structurally different tasks. This suggests that the integration of bi-directional attention between text and structure fosters embeddings that are robust, flexible, and broadly applicable across multiple graph-based learning scenarios. To gain further insight into learned embeddings, we performed clustering. The results and analysis are available in Appendix B. Accuracy is used as the primary evaluation metric for this dataset. OGBN-Arxiv is a large-scale citation network sourced from the Open Graph Benchmark [33] (OGB). Each node represents a scientific paper from the computer science domain posted on arXiv, and edges correspond to citation links directed from newer papers to older ones. The graph includes approximately 169,343 nodes and 1.1 million edges. The task is to predict the subject category of each paper among 40 classes. The standard data split provided by OGB is used, and node classification accuracy serves as the evaluation metric. OGBN-Products (subset) [33] is an undirected product co-purchasing network derived from Amazon. In this graph, each node represents a product, and an edge connects two products if they are frequently bought together. The dataset contains approximately 54,025 nodes and 74,420 edges. The classification task involves predicting the product category among 47 distinct classes. The dataset follows the official split provided by the Open Graph Benchmark, and accuracy is used as the primary evaluation measure. Arxiv2023 [7] is a curated citation network derived from a subset of arXiv papers published in 2023.

Each node corresponds to a scientific article, and directed edges reflect citation links among them. The dataset is designed specifically for evaluating text-attributed graph models, where each node is associated with rich textual metadata such as the title and abstract. The task is node classification into a predefined set of subject areas, with performance measured using accuracy.

div.head: B Node Clustering as an Unsupervised Evaluation
div.p: To further assess the semantic coherence and class-separability encoded in the learned node embeddings, we conducted an unsupervised node clustering experiment on the OGBN-Arxiv and Arxiv2023. Specifically, we applied K-Means clustering to the embeddings generated by our model (BiGTex), as well as those from two baseline feature Original and SimTeG. We set the number of clusters equal to the number of ground-truth classes (i.e., 40 for OGBN-Arxiv) and evaluated the clustering performance using two standard metrics:
1. Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) [44], which measures the similarity between clustering assignments and ground-truth labels, adjusted for chance.
2. Normalized Mutual Information (NMI) [45], which captures the amount of mutual information shared between the clustering output and the true class distribution.
As shown in Table 6, our model outperforms all other baselines in both ARI and NMI. Notably, it achieves an ARI of 0.4115 and NMI of 0.6679, significantly surpassing both Original and TAPE. Even
